seoTitle: How to Create a Jira Workflow
metaDescription: Learn how to build a custom Jira workflow with your own statuses and transitions so issues move the way your team actually works.
intro: Jira's default workflow doesn't always match how your team really moves work forward. Creating a custom workflow lets you define the exact statuses and transitions issues go through, from creation to done. Here's how to set one up.

steps.heading: Open workflow settings
steps.body: Go to project or global settings and find the Workflows section where existing workflows are listed.

steps.heading: Create a new workflow
steps.body: Start a new workflow from scratch or copy an existing one as a starting point to modify.

steps.heading: Add your statuses
steps.body: Define each status the issue should pass through, such as To Do, In Review, Blocked, or Done.

steps.heading: Define transitions between statuses
steps.body: Draw the allowed transitions between statuses so issues can only move along the paths you approve.

steps.heading: Publish and assign the workflow
steps.body: Publish the workflow and associate it with the relevant project and issue types so it takes effect.

faq.question: Can I edit a workflow that's already in use?
faq.answer: Yes, but changes need to be published, and Jira will walk you through migrating any issues affected by removed statuses.

faq.question: Do different issue types need different workflows?
faq.answer: They can; many teams use separate workflows for bugs, stories, and epics to reflect different processes.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many statuses I can add?
faq.answer: There's no strict limit, but workflows with too many statuses tend to become harder for teams to follow consistently.

faq.question: Can I restrict who can move an issue between statuses?
faq.answer: Yes, transitions can have conditions and permissions attached so only certain roles can trigger them.
